fingerprint.hero_desc: Discover what information websites can silently collect about your browser. Scan for fingerprinting techniques, WebRTC leaks, and privacy vulnerabilities in seconds.
fingerprint.about_desc: A browser fingerprint is a unique set of characteristics that identifies your browser across websites, even without cookies. It includes your screen resolution, installed fonts, WebGL renderer, canvas rendering, audio processing, and dozens of other attributes.
fingerprint.tip1: Use a privacy-focused browser like Tor or Brave
fingerprint.tip3: Use extensions to block fingerprinting scripts

fingerprint.privacy.label: Privacy
fingerprint.privacy.title: Your privacy is our top priority
fingerprint.privacy.body1: Unlike many online fingerprinting tools that send your data to remote servers for analysis, our scanner runs 100% in your browser using JavaScript APIs. No data is transmitted, stored, or logged at any point during the scan.
fingerprint.privacy.body2: All fingerprinting detection happens locally on your device. We do not use cookies, analytics tracking, or any form of data collection. Your results are generated instantly and remain entirely private.
fingerprint.about.label: About
fingerprint.about.seo.title: Understanding browser fingerprinting and how to protect your privacy online
fingerprint.about.body1: Browser fingerprinting is a sophisticated tracking technique that websites and advertising networks use to identify individual visitors without relying on cookies or other traditional storage methods. When you visit a website, your browser automatically shares a remarkable amount of technical information about your device and software configuration. This includes details such as your operating system, browser type and version, screen resolution, color depth, installed fonts, hardware capabilities, language preferences, timezone settings, WebGL renderer information, Canvas rendering output, audio processing characteristics, and many other attributes. Individually, each data point may seem harmless, but when combined they create a highly unique digital signature that can distinguish your browser from millions of others with remarkable accuracy. Studies have shown that browser fingerprints can uniquely identify over 80 percent of browsers even when cookies and other traditional tracking methods are disabled.
fingerprint.about.body2: The practical implications of browser fingerprinting are significant for everyday internet users. Advertisers and data brokers use fingerprinting to build detailed profiles of your browsing behavior across multiple websites, enabling targeted advertising and behavioral tracking that persists even when you clear cookies or use private browsing mode. Social media platforms and analytics companies employ these techniques to link anonymous browsing sessions to known user identities. Even government surveillance programs have been documented using fingerprinting as part of their monitoring toolkit. Unlike cookies, which users can delete or block, browser fingerprints are difficult to detect and even harder to prevent because they rely on legitimate browser APIs that are necessary for modern web functionality. Many users are completely unaware that fingerprinting is happening, as it occurs silently in the background without any visible indicators.
fingerprint.about.body3: Fortunately, there are several effective strategies you can employ to reduce your fingerprintability and protect your online privacy. Using privacy-focused browsers such as Tor Browser or Brave can significantly reduce fingerprinting surface by standardizing or randomizing common fingerprinting vectors. Installing browser extensions like Privacy Badger, Canvas Blocker, or uBlock Origin can block known fingerprinting scripts and randomize canvas and WebGL outputs. Disabling WebRTC in your browser settings prevents real-time communication features from leaking your local IP addresses. Regularly updating your browser ensures you benefit from the latest privacy protections that browser vendors implement. Using a virtual private network adds an additional layer of anonymity by masking your IP address. For maximum protection, combining multiple privacy tools and maintaining good security hygiene creates a layered defense that makes fingerprint-based tracking considerably more difficult. Our scanner helps you understand your current exposure level so you can take informed action to protect your digital identity.
fingerprint.faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
fingerprint.faq.a1: A browser fingerprint is a unique identifier created from the combination of your browser's technical attributes such as screen resolution, installed fonts, WebGL renderer, canvas rendering output, audio processing signature, and other device characteristics. This fingerprint can identify you across websites even without cookies.
fingerprint.faq.a2: Browser fingerprinting is remarkably accurate. Research by the Electronic Frontier Foundation found that 83.6 percent of browsers had a unique fingerprint among over 290,000 tested samples. With modern techniques, this accuracy has increased further, making fingerprinting one of the most effective cross-site tracking methods available.
fingerprint.faq.q3: Can I prevent browser fingerprinting completely?
fingerprint.faq.a3: Complete prevention is extremely difficult because fingerprinting relies on standard browser APIs required for web functionality. However, you can significantly reduce your fingerprint uniqueness by using privacy-focused browsers like Tor, installing anti-fingerprinting extensions, disabling JavaScript, and standardizing your browser configuration to blend in with the crowd.
fingerprint.faq.q4: Is my data sent to any server during the scan?
fingerprint.faq.a4: No. Our fingerprint scanner runs entirely in your browser using client-side JavaScript. No data is transmitted to any server, nothing is stored or logged, and your results are generated locally. This is why we emphasize that the tool is 100 percent client-side.
fingerprint.faq.q5: What is a WebRTC leak and why is it dangerous?
fingerprint.faq.a5: WebRTC is a browser feature that enables real-time voice and video communication. However, it can inadvertently expose your real IP address, including local network addresses, even when you are using a VPN or proxy. This leak undermines your anonymity and allows websites to determine your actual location and network configuration.
fingerprint.history_empty: No scan history yet. Run a scan to see results here.
fingerprint.rec.score_unique: Your fingerprint is {score}% unique. Websites can track you across sessions even without cookies.
fingerprint.rec.fonts_count: {count} fonts detected. The combination is a strong tracking signal.
fingerprint.rec.plugins_count: {count} plugins visible. Modern browsers should not expose these.
fingerprint.rec.webrtc.title: WebRTC IP Leak Detected
fingerprint.rec.webrtc.desc: Your real IP address is leaking through WebRTC, even if you use a VPN or proxy.
fingerprint.rec.webrtc.fix: Install uBlock Origin or Privacy Badger and enable WebRTC leak prevention. Firefox: about:config → media.peerconnection.enabled → false. Chrome: use "WebRTC Leak Prevent" extension.
fingerprint.rec.canvas.title: Canvas Fingerprint is Unique
fingerprint.rec.canvas.desc: Websites can identify you by how your browser renders images on a canvas element.
fingerprint.rec.canvas.fix: Install "Canvas Blocker" extension to randomize canvas output. Or use Brave/Tor Browser with built-in canvas protection.
fingerprint.rec.webgl.title: WebGL Hardware Info Exposed
fingerprint.rec.webgl.desc: Your GPU vendor and renderer info are visible, creating a highly unique hardware fingerprint.
fingerprint.rec.webgl.fix: Use "Canvas Blocker" to spoof WebGL data. Brave provides built-in fingerprint randomization. Tor Browser blocks it entirely.
fingerprint.rec.unique.title: Browser is Highly Identifiable
fingerprint.rec.unique.desc: Your browser fingerprint is very unique, making you easily trackable across websites.
fingerprint.rec.unique.fix: Use Tor Browser for maximum anonymity. For daily browsing: Brave with "Strict" fingerprint blocking, or Firefox + uBlock Origin + Privacy Badger + Canvas Blocker.
fingerprint.rec.fonts.title: Font List is Identifying
fingerprint.rec.fonts.desc: Your installed font list is a strong fingerprint signal.
fingerprint.rec.fonts.fix: Use a browser with font randomization (Brave, Tor). "Canvas Blocker" extension can also mask font enumeration.
fingerprint.rec.audio.title: Audio Fingerprint Detected
fingerprint.rec.audio.desc: Your audio hardware produces a unique signature for cross-site tracking.
fingerprint.rec.audio.fix: Audio fingerprinting is hard to block. Tor Browser or Brave with strict settings provides the best protection. "AudioContext Fingerprint Defender" adds random noise.
fingerprint.rec.plugins.title: Browser Plugins Detected
fingerprint.rec.plugins.desc: Your browser exposes plugin information that can be used for tracking.
fingerprint.rec.plugins.fix: Update your browser to the latest version. Chrome 90+ and Firefox 85+ disable plugin enumeration by default.
fingerprint.rec.general.title: General Privacy Recommendations
fingerprint.rec.general.desc: Basic steps everyone should take for better browser privacy.
fingerprint.rec.general.fix: 1) Use privacy browser (Brave/Firefox). 2) Install uBlock Origin. 3) Use a VPN. 4) Disable third-party cookies. 5) Enable HTTPS-only mode. 6) Review browser permissions.
fingerprint.rec.fix.clear_body: This will clear cookies, localStorage, and sessionStorage for this site. This removes stored tracking data but will not affect your browser fingerprint.
fingerprint.rec.fix.ext_body: You will be redirected to the extension store. Install the recommended extension and restart your browser for the fix to take effect.
fingerprint.rec.fix.settings_body: Click confirm to open your browser's update/settings page. Keeping your browser up to date ensures the latest privacy protections are active.
browser-fingerprint.seo.article:
Browser Fingerprint Scanner — Detect 12+ Tracking Vectors Instantly

Want to know how much personal information websites can extract from your browser? Our browser fingerprint scanner detects WebRTC leaks, Canvas fingerprints, WebGL hardware details, font enumeration, audio signatures, and 7 more tracking techniques in seconds. Understand your current privacy exposure and get targeted protection recommendations.

What Is Browser Fingerprinting and Why Should You Care?

Browser fingerprinting is a sophisticated tracking technique that allows websites and advertising networks to identify you uniquely without using cookies or any stored data on your device. Unlike traditional tracking methods that you can defeat by clearing your browser history or using incognito mode, fingerprinting works silently in the background and is extremely difficult to detect or prevent.

Every time you visit a website, your browser automatically shares a wealth of technical information about your device and configuration. This includes your screen resolution, color depth, installed fonts, GPU model, Canvas rendering output, audio processing characteristics, timezone settings, language preferences, CPU core count, memory size, and dozens of other attributes. Individually, each data point seems harmless. But when combined together, they create a highly unique digital signature. Research by the Electronic Frontier Foundation found that over 83% of browsers have a unique fingerprint, even with cookies and JavaScript disabled.

⚠️ Browser fingerprinting operates silently without any visible indicators. You may be tracked right now without knowing it.

The implications are far-reaching. Advertisers use fingerprinting to build detailed profiles of your browsing behavior across multiple websites, enabling targeted advertising that persists even when you clear cookies. Data brokers collect and sell these profiles to third parties. Social media platforms link anonymous browsing sessions to your real identity using fingerprinting techniques. Even government surveillance programs have been documented using fingerprinting as part of their monitoring operations. The key difference between fingerprinting and cookie-based tracking is that fingerprints cannot be deleted, blocked, or opted out of through traditional privacy settings.

Who Needs a Browser Fingerprint Check?

Whether you are a casual internet user or a cybersecurity professional, understanding your browser fingerprint exposure is essential for protecting your digital privacy:

  • Everyday Internet Users: Wondering if your browser is leaking personal data? A single scan reveals how much information websites can silently collect about you. Understanding your exposure level is the first step toward better privacy protection.
  • Privacy Enthusiasts: Already using a VPN and privacy extensions? Fingerprint testing validates whether these tools actually work as advertised. Many VPNs cannot prevent WebRTC IP leaks — our scanner identifies these blind spots instantly.
  • VPN Users: Even with an active VPN connection, the WebRTC feature in your browser may leak your real IP address. Our tool specifically detects this type of leak, ensuring your anonymity remains intact.
  • Enterprise Security Teams: Evaluate employee browser security configurations, develop privacy protection policies, and use the scanner as a demonstration tool for security awareness training sessions.
  • Web Developers: Test tracking scripts on your websites, verify anti-fingerprinting implementations, and debug Canvas and WebGL rendering code with detailed fingerprint data.
  • Security Researchers: Study fingerprinting differences across browsers, evaluate the effectiveness of privacy-focused browsers, and analyze how tracking techniques evolve over time.

How to Check Your Browser Fingerprint — 3 Simple Steps

  1. Click Start Scan — Launch a comprehensive fingerprint analysis with a single click. No software installation or browser extensions required. All detection runs entirely within your browser using local JavaScript APIs. No data is ever sent to external servers.
  2. Automatic Deep Scan — The scanner automatically checks 12+ tracking vectors including browser information, WebRTC leaks, Canvas fingerprints, WebGL hardware data, installed fonts, screen resolution and timezone, language settings, plugins, audio signatures, hardware details, storage capabilities, and network status. The entire process takes only a few seconds.
  3. Get Your Protection Plan — After scanning completes, you will see your browser uniqueness score, risk levels for each detection category, and targeted fix recommendations. Install recommended browser extensions with one click to enhance privacy, or download a complete detection report for further analysis.

Detection Vectors Explained — Risk Levels and What They Mean

Our scanner covers the following detection dimensions, each carrying a different privacy risk level:

  • WebRTC IP Leak (High Risk): WebRTC enables real-time communication in browsers, but it can silently leak your real IP address even when you are using a VPN or proxy. This is one of the most serious privacy vulnerabilities, as it directly reveals your network location.
  • Canvas Fingerprint (High Risk): Websites instruct your browser to render hidden images on a Canvas element, then analyze the subtle differences in rendering output to create a unique identifier. Different hardware, drivers, and graphics settings produce distinct rendering results that are nearly impossible to disguise without specialized tools.
  • WebGL Hardware Info (Medium Risk): WebGL exposes your GPU vendor and renderer model, such as "NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090" or "Apple M2 GPU." This information can precisely narrow down your device configuration and is particularly identifying for users with high-end or unusual hardware.
  • Installed Fonts (Medium Risk): By detecting which fonts are installed on your system, websites can create a unique software configuration signature. Users who have installed many fonts or specialized typefaces are especially easy to identify through this method.
  • Audio Fingerprint (Medium Risk): The AudioContext API reveals characteristics of your audio hardware and drivers. Different sound cards and audio processors produce unique processing signatures that can be used for cross-site tracking.
  • Screen and Timezone (Low Risk): Screen resolution, color depth, pixel ratio, and timezone settings provide basic device information. While low risk individually, these data points become identifying when combined with other fingerprint vectors.
  • Language and Locale (Low Risk): Browser language preferences and locale settings are standard information shared by all browsers, but they gain identifying power among multilingual users or those with uncommon language combinations.
  • Hardware Info (Low Risk): CPU core count, device memory, and touch point information. Modern browsers are increasingly limiting exposure of these hardware details, making this a lower-risk vector over time.

Browser Privacy Comparison — Which Browser Protects You Best?

Choosing the right browser is the single most impactful decision you can make to reduce your fingerprint identifiability. Here is how major browsers compare in fingerprint protection:

  • Tor Browser (Score: 98/100): All Tor users share a highly uniform browser fingerprint, making individual tracking extremely difficult. It disables WebRTC by default, randomizes Canvas output, and standardizes window sizes. This is the top choice for maximum anonymity.
  • Brave (Score: 85/100): Brave offers a "Strict" fingerprint blocking mode that randomizes Canvas, WebGL, and audio fingerprint outputs. It also includes built-in ad blocking and HTTPS upgrades, making it the best privacy choice for everyday browsing without sacrificing usability.
  • Firefox (Score: 70/100): Firefox Enhanced Tracking Protection provides basic fingerprint defense. When combined with extensions like uBlock Origin, Canvas Blocker, and Privacy Badger, Firefox achieves strong privacy protection. Manual configuration is required for optimal results.
  • Chrome (Score: 35/100): As the browser with the largest market share, Chrome has the widest fingerprint attack surface. It lacks built-in fingerprint protection features and relies entirely on third-party extensions for privacy enhancement.
  • Edge (Score: 30/100): Edge shares the Chromium engine with Chrome, resulting in similar fingerprint exposure levels without additional privacy protections.

💡 Tip: Regardless of which browser you use, running regular fingerprint checks helps you stay informed about your current privacy status and quickly identify security vulnerabilities.

Why Choose Our Browser Fingerprint Scanner?

While several online fingerprint testing tools exist, our scanner offers distinct advantages:

  • 100% Client-Side Processing: Every detection runs in your browser using JavaScript APIs. No data is transmitted, stored, or logged at any point during the scanning process. Your privacy is always protected.
  • Comprehensive 12+ Vector Coverage: From WebRTC leaks to audio fingerprints, Canvas rendering to hardware details — we cover all major browser fingerprinting techniques without missing any risk points.
  • Instant Results with Scoring: See your browser uniqueness score immediately after scanning. The score intuitively shows how unique you are among millions of users — a higher score means greater tracking risk.
  • Actionable Fix Recommendations: We do not just identify problems — we provide specific solutions. Each detected risk includes a one-click fix button that takes you directly to the extension store or browser settings page.
  • Scan History Tracking: Automatically saves scan results to local storage, allowing you to compare fingerprint changes across different time points or configurations and track your privacy improvement over time.
  • Completely Free, No Registration: No account creation, no email required, no software downloads. Open the page and start scanning immediately.
  • Multi-Language Support: Available in English, Chinese, Japanese, Korean, and Spanish. All detection results and recommendations are presented in your preferred language.

Check Your Browser Fingerprint Now

In just a few seconds, discover how much personal information your browser is exposing. The scan is completely free and your data never leaves your browser.
